---
title: "Packages, algorithms and help"
subtitle: "Which tool for which task — and what is available on DST"
date: last-modified
---

This is a reference hub: which tool solves which task, whether it is available on DST, and where to find more help.
The guide takes you to an analysis-ready dataset and points you towards the right tools for the actual analysis — but does **not teach the statistical methods**. For that you need a statistician, a course, or the packages' own documentation.

---

## 1. Validated algorithms

Published, peer-reviewed methods you can use directly to construct covariates.
Code for NMI and the SEPLINE algorithm is under development.

::: {.wide-first-col}

| Algorithm | What it does | Article |
|---|---|---|
| **Open Source Diabetes Classifier (OSDC)** | Classifies everyone in Denmark as T1D, T2D or no diabetes | [doi:10.2147/CLEP.S407019](https://doi.org/10.2147/CLEP.S407019) |
| **Nordic Multimorbidity Index (NMI)** | Weighted comorbidity score (50 predictors) | [doi:10.2147/CLEP.S353398](https://doi.org/10.2147/CLEP.S353398) |
| **SEPLINE — Socioeconomic Position in Epidemiological Research** | National guideline for socioeconomic variables | [doi:10.2147/CLEP.S520772](https://doi.org/10.2147/CLEP.S520772) |

:::

- **OSDC** — define a diabetes population, type and onset date → [Phase 13 — OSDC](13b_osdc.qmd)
- **NMI** — adjust for comorbidity in a model or describe comorbidity burden → [Phase 13 — NMI](13c_nmi.qmd)
- **SEPLINE** — education, income, employment as covariates → [Phase 12 — Socioeconomic variables](12_socioekonomiske-variable.qmd)

::: {.callout-tip}
**Charlson as an alternative to NMI:** both `heaven::charlsonIndex()` and `EpiForsk` (see below) calculate Charlson comorbidity score from LPR diagnoses — both are on DST.
:::

---

## 2. Existing functions for data management and extraction

### `heaven` — Danish register data toolkit
*(pre-installed on DST; not on CRAN — [github.com/tagteam/heaven](https://github.com/tagteam/heaven))*

Functions built specifically for Danish register data by Thomas Gerds (University of Copenhagen, Biostatistics): Lexis-splitting for time-varying covariates, drug exposure windows from LMDB, risk-set matching and Charlson score.

<details>
<summary>What heaven can do — and when to use it</summary>

**Lexis-splitting** — split follow-up time across multiple time axes when a covariate changes over time (age, calendar period, time since exposure).

```r
library(heaven)

df_split <- lexisTwo(
  indat      = kohort,
  invars     = c("pnr", "inn", "out", "event"),   # id, start date, end date, outcome
  splitvars  = list(age = c(40, 50, 60, 70),       # age boundaries
                    cal = c(2010, 2015, 2020))      # calendar years
)
# Returns: one data frame with MORE rows per person — one per time interval.
# New columns are added for the current bands (age group, calendar period etc.).
# Ready for a Cox model with time-varying covariates.
```

**Drug exposure from LMDB** — calculate exposure periods from prescription data (e.g. "was the person on metformin at index date?").

```r
exp_table <- medicinMacro(
  indat = lmdb_data, id = "pnr", atc = "atc", eksd = "eksd",
  ddds = "ddds", maxdepot = 3
)
# Returns: one row per exposure period per person with start and end date
# for the period. Use subsequent joins to determine exposure at index date.
```

**Charlson comorbidity score** from LPR diagnoses:

```r
charlson_data <- charlsonIndex(indat = lpr_diag_data, id = "pnr",
                               code = "c_diag", code_type = "icd10")
# Returns: one row per person with total Charlson score and binary indicators
# per Charlson disease group. The function matches ICD-10 codes to categories itself.
```

**Risk-set matching** — match cases to controls at index date:

```r
matched <- riskSetMatch(ptid = "pnr", event = "event",
                        terms = c("koen", "birth_year"), dat = kohort, ratio = 5)
# Returns: matched dataset with matchID linking cases and controls.
```

::: {.callout-note}
`heaven` is confirmed available on DST — use `library(heaven)` directly. It is not on CRAN, so outside DST it must be fetched from [GitHub](https://github.com/tagteam/heaven).
:::

</details>

---

### `EpiForsk` — register data helper functions from SSI
*(CRAN; on DST — [github.com/Laksafoss/EpiForsk](https://github.com/Laksafoss/EpiForsk))*

Code-sharing package from the Statens Serum Institut. Particularly useful: `flatten_date_intervals()` which merges overlapping hospital contacts into continuous periods — necessary before comorbidity lookback. Danish Charlson score included.

<details>
<summary>Most important functions and when to use them</summary>

**`flatten_date_intervals()` — overlapping contacts**

A person can have two overlapping admissions in LPR (e.g. a transfer). Before comorbidity lookback, overlapping periods must be merged:

```r
library(EpiForsk)

lpr_flat <- flatten_date_intervals(
  data = lpr_contacts, id = "pnr",
  start_date = "d_inddto", end_date = "d_uddto"
)
# Returns: a data frame with merged start/end per person —
# overlapping periods are reduced to the total covered period.
```

**`charlson_score()` — Danish-calibrated Charlson comorbidity score** from ICD-10 (incl. D-prefix). See the callout below for what it requires.

```r
charlson <- charlson_score(data = lpr_diag_data, id = "pnr", icd_codes = "c_diag")
# Returns: one row per person with Charlson score.
```

::: {.callout-important}
**What does `charlson_score()` require?**
You must extract a lookback period from LPR yourself (typically 5 years before index date) and give the function **all diagnoses** in that window — A, B and G type. You do **not** need to filter on specific ICD codes in advance; the function matches the codes to Charlson categories itself. The D-prefix (`DG30` etc.) is handled by EpiForsk — it does not need to be stripped first.

```r
# Example: extract lookback and pass all diagnoses
# pnr and d_inddto are in lpr_adm — not lpr_diag

# Step 1: broadest possible time window for the entire cohort
min_lookback <- min(kohort$index_date) - 365.25 * 5   # earliest lookback start

# Step 2: fetch contacts and join to diagnoses
lpr2_lookback <- lpr_adm %>%
  filter(pnr %in% !!kohort$pnr,
         d_inddto >= !!min_lookback) %>%              # broad pre-filter
  select(pnr, recnum, d_inddto) %>%
  inner_join(
    lpr_diag %>%
      filter(c_diagtype %in% c("A", "B", "G")) %>%   # A/B/G for comorbidity
      select(recnum, c_diag),
    by = "recnum"
  ) %>%
  collect() %>%
  left_join(kohort %>% select(pnr, index_date), by = "pnr") %>%
  filter(d_inddto >= index_date - 365.25 * 5,        # precise per-person lookback
         d_inddto < index_date)

charlson <- charlson_score(data = lpr2_lookback, id = "pnr", icd_codes = "c_diag")
```
:::

</details>

---

### `dataReporter` — automatic data quality report {#datareporter-automatic-data-quality-report}
*(CRAN; confirmed on DST — [github.com/ekstroem/dataReporter](https://github.com/ekstroem/dataReporter))*

Automatically generates an HTML/PDF report of all columns in a dataset: distribution, missing values, outliers and type checks. Useful for validating a new register extraction before starting analysis. Published in Journal of Statistical Software.

```r
library(dataReporter)
makeDataReport(my_extract)   # opens report in browser
```

See also [Phase 7 — Inspect your data](07_inspicer-data.qmd) for manual inspection commands.

---

### `fakeregs` — synthetic Danish register data
*(GitHub only — local use only — [github.com/steno-aarhus/fakeregs](https://github.com/steno-aarhus/fakeregs))*

Fictitious but structurally realistic data for nine DST registers. For practising and testing code locally *without* DST access. Not used on the server. See [Phase 6 — First extraction](06_foerste-udtraek.qmd).

---

### `fastreg` — SAS → parquet
*(GitHub only — [github.com/dp-next/fastreg](https://github.com/dp-next/fastreg))*

Structured pipeline for converting an entire SAS workspace to parquet. Only relevant if you are building a parquet workspace from scratch. See [Phase 4 — SAS → parquet](04_filformater.qmd#convert-sas-to-parquet).

---

## 3. Table 1 — baseline characteristics

Table 1 summarises your study population before analysis. `gtsummary` is the standard package.

```r
library(gtsummary)   # tbl_summary(), tbl_merge()

# Assumes cohort has columns: group, alder, koen, nmi_score, occupation_cat
table1 <- kohort %>%
  select(group, alder, koen, nmi_score, occupation_cat) %>%   # select variables for the table
  tbl_summary(
    by = group,                                                # split by exposed vs comparator cohort
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous()  ~ "{mean} ({sd})",                    # continuous: mean (SD)
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"                        # categorical: n (%)
    ),
    missing = "no"                                             # do not show NA rows separately
  ) %>%
  add_overall() %>%                                            # add an overall column
  add_p()                                                      # p-values for group differences
```

::: {.callout-warning}
**Output control:** Counts of 0–4 may not be repatriated. Round to the nearest 5 with `round_5_gtsummary()` from [`Z.gtsummary.addons`](https://github.com/zheer-kejlberg/Z.gtsummary.addons) — the package is not on DST and must be imported via your data manager.
Manual rounding: `round(x / 5) * 5` gives the nearest 5.
:::

---

## Analysis: regression, survival and competing risks

These are pointers only — the guide does not teach the methods. All of the following are **confirmed on DST** (June 2026).

::: {.wide-first-col}

| Package | Use it for | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **[`prodlim`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=prodlim)** — Product-Limit Estimation | Kaplan–Meier and **Aalen–Johansen** cumulative incidence | Core — correct descriptive curve when death is a competing risk (KM alone overestimates) |
| **[`riskRegression`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=riskRegression)** | Cause-Specific Cox (`CSC`), Fine-Gray regression (`FGR`), absolute risk (`predictRisk`), model validation (`Score`: Brier, time-dependent AUC) | Core — main analysis: dementia with death as competing risk |
| **[`Publish`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=Publish)** | `regressionTable()` → HR tables with confidence intervals, `plotConfidence()` forest plots | Core — Table 2 quickly |
| **[`gtsummary`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=gtsummary)** | `tbl_summary()` Table 1, `tbl_regression()` Cox/logistic, `tbl_survfit()` survival summary | Widely used standard for manuscript tables (R Journal 2021) |
| **[`MatchIt`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=MatchIt)** | Propensity score + exact + optimal matching, balance diagnostics (Love plots) | Alternative to `heaven::riskSetMatch()` — better for PS-matching with balance check |
| **[`pec`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=pec)** — Prediction Error Curves | Prediction error curves (Brier over time) | Situational — only for prediction models; `riskRegression::Score()` often covers the need |
| **[`BuyseTest`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=BuyseTest)** | Generalised Pairwise Comparisons: win ratio, net benefit, prioritised endpoints | Primarily **Study 2 (T1D)**; niche for dementia |

:::

All packages are documented at [tagteam (Thomas Gerds, KU)](https://github.com/tagteam) and [bozenne (Brice Ozenne, KU)](https://github.com/bozenne/BuyseTest).

### Possible inspiration — not available on DST

::: {.callout-note}
The packages below are **not available on DST** (GitHub-only; require import via data manager) and are not part of the recommended workflow. They are mentioned as inspiration — check yourself whether they are relevant for your project.
:::

**[`plotsurv`](https://github.com/zheer-kejlberg/plotsurv)** — publication-ready Kaplan–Meier and Aalen–Johansen competing-risks plots with at-risk table, CI bands and censoring ticks. `plotsurv(survfit_object)`.

**[`Z.gtsummary.addons`](https://github.com/zheer-kejlberg/Z.gtsummary.addons)** — extends `gtsummary` with `add_SMD()` (standardised mean differences for Table 1) and `round_5_gtsummary()` (rounds counts to the nearest 5 — relevant for DST output control).

**[`tame`](https://github.com/Laksafoss/tame)** *(CRAN, but not confirmed on DST)* — clusters longitudinal medication patterns from LMDB on ATC, timing and dose. Relevant for characterising polypharmacy patterns.

---

## 4. Where to get more help?

**Community and courses** — see the prioritised help list and course links in [Phase 2 — R: the bare essentials](02_r-intro.qmd) (Stack Overflow, Zheers R Coding Café, DDEA, R for Data Science, Epi R Handbook).

**Institutional reference material:**

| Source | What you find |
|---|---|
| [ctpteam/RDST](https://github.com/ctpteam/RDST) | "R on DST" manual: project setup, data access, good coding practice on the DST server |
| [ctpteam/DST](https://github.com/ctpteam/DST) | Guide to LPR3, DST manuals, slides and a heaven PDF |
| [steno-aarhus/registers-project-database](https://steno-aarhus.github.io/registers-project-database/) | SDCA's own database: variable lists, register descriptions and application templates for the Danish Health Data Authority |
| [github.com/tagteam](https://github.com/tagteam) | Documentation for `riskRegression`, `prodlim`, `Publish`, `pec`, `heaven` |

---

## Next steps

→ [Phase 15 — Export and repatriation](15_eksport-hjemsendelse.qmd)
